rationale: Agrawal et al. (2007) is cited in the core theory development, not merely as general background. The citing paper uses it to motivate discrete-emotion compatibility effects and to represent a prior emotional-overload explanation for why compatible negative appeals can reduce persuasion. The 2010 article then argues for and tests a different process in this setting - defensive processing and emotion repair for shame and guilt. Because it preserves the core phenomenon but re-specifies its mechanism in a narrower context, this is a substantive refinement.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Agrawal et al. (2007) as prior evidence on emotion-message compatibility, especially its overload-based interpretation under negative emotions. It refines that account by showing that, for shame/guilt antidrinking appeals, compatibility backfires through defensive processing and subsequent emotion repair rather than simple processing shutdown.
